# The Architecture 2.0 Lifecycle {#sec-architecture-20-ontology}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"All models are wrong, but some are useful."*
>
> — George E. P. Box, *Robustness in the Strategy of Scientific Model Building* (1979) [@Box1979Robustness]
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** George E. P. Box, a British statistician, coined this aphorism about statistical models. Every model simplifies, and the working question is whether the simplification still serves the decision at hand. His point applies to more than prediction models. A task description, benchmark, simulator, power model, or test suite always leaves something out. Each can still be useful when its scope and failure modes are clear and when the decision does not reach beyond the evidence it provides.
:::


::: {.callout-crux}
How do we structure our hypotheses, constraints, and toolchains so that an AI agent can execute an architecture study without silently violating the physical and logical realities of the system?
:::

The preceding chapters established a hard reality: modern architecture spaces have grown too complex for us to explore manually, yet unguided AI risks optimizing proxy metrics at the expense of physical silicon constraints. We cannot simply hand over a prompt and expect a taped-out chip in return. To safely delegate exploration to an AI agent, the tacit human knowledge we naturally carry—such as implicit workload expectations, unstated thermal limits, and trusted tool combinations—must become explicit, machine-readable state.

This requires a formal ontology: a conceptual model of our work that strictly defines what an agent can see, what it can change, and how we will judge its results. Without this structure, we risk building AI systems that hallucinate successful simulations or optimize for the wrong goals entirely. By formalizing our intent and constraints, we turn stochastic models from autonomous novelties into accountable participants in our design loop.

This chapter establishes the Architecture 2.0 lifecycle, a structured engineering discipline for AI-assisted architecture. We establish the bounded architecture study as the fundamental unit of work, ensuring that every AI-driven exploration remains anchored to a testable claim. We then introduce the six-stage lifecycle that carries this claim from formulation to defense, ensuring our design loop remains inspectable, rigorous, and firmly under our control.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After reading this chapter, you can:

- Formulate bounded architecture studies by specifying explicit design-space constraints, baseline workloads, and verifiable claims that transform tacit hardware knowledge into machine-readable state.
- Structure multi-fidelity design-space exploration using explicit escalation policies and standardized tool interfaces to reconcile rapid proxy estimates with high-fidelity simulation and physical synthesis constraints.
- Differentiate genuine architectural improvements from surrogate model artifacts by applying causal simulator ablations and recording negative evidence throughout evaluation.
- Establish formal decision-rights matrices and design-loop cards to partition automated agentic execution from non-delegable human sign-off authority.
:::

While this chapter provides the high-level structural overview of the lifecycle, the exact implementation details—including how to build state representations, search algorithms, robust tool interfaces, verification techniques, and accountability structures—are covered in greater detail in the subsequent chapters.

## The Big Picture: Translating Intent to Execution {#sec-arch2-ontology}

An AI-assisted architecture study needs a common structure connecting the stages of work that are often documented separately. It must preserve our original intent, define a bounded comparison, connect that comparison to an inspectable execution loop, and show who may act on the result.

@fig-structured-layer-above-rtl places an architecture study between our question and the tools used to investigate it. The figure's middle layer is the comparison and its review record. It connects reasoning that often stays in our heads or working notes to executable implementation flows, giving architectural tools, AI agents, reviewers, and architects a shared question for interpreting each artifact and tool result.

![**An AI-assisted architecture study connects intent to implementation evidence.** The study contains the scope, architectural claim, execution and analysis process, and evidence needed for review. Iterative work uses a design loop.](images/F4d-structured-layer-above-rtl){#fig-structured-layer-above-rtl width="85%" fig-alt="Three-layer diagram showing architecture intent and judgment flowing into a structured architecture study, with tool results flowing back for evidence review."}

Read @fig-structured-layer-above-rtl from top to bottom:

* **The Upper Layer (System Intent)**: This contains the architecture models, requirements, and rationale that shape our question. For the RISC-V XR subsystem from our Lighthouse prompt, this includes the high-level goal of improving mobile XR efficiency and the initial models of the target workload.
* **The Middle Layer (Bounded Comparison)**: This turns intent into a bounded comparison. Its scope names the specific question, such as deciding whether a particular cache configuration merits RTL evaluation. The architectural claim says what will be reviewed. It is not another architecture tool; rather, it connects design intent to tool execution.
* **The Lower Layer (Executable Tool Path)**: Simulators, compilers, RTL generators, and EDA flows carry out different parts of the comparison to return measurements that enter the evidence record.

In software engineering, coding agents rely on structured environments like SWE-bench to provide a bounded issue, a known repository state, and executable tests. Architecture requires a similar structure, but building it is much harder because feedback (power, area, timing) is delayed, ambiguous, and subject to physical constraints.

Because architecture state is scattered and tool feedback carries different costs and fidelity, an AI-assisted architecture study requires more than just an environment. It requires a bounded claim, evidence at the right fidelity, and a named person who owns the final decision.

## The Six-Stage Execution Lifecycle

To execute the work within this ontology, we must formally define the iterative process where architectural decisions unfold. Experienced architects already move through these stages informally. When we run the work ourselves, we naturally carry project context from a spreadsheet to a simulator to a design review.

But why is it so important to break these standard stages down so formally? The answer is that human architects rely on vast amounts of *tacit knowledge*—implicit workload expectations, unstated thermal limits, and trusted tool combinations. An AI agent cannot. If we do not make this implicit workflow explicit, an agent will blindly optimize the metric it was given, entirely unaware that it just proposed a design that is physically unroutable or thermally destructive. Formalizing the lifecycle is the only way to convert our tacit engineering boundaries into a machine-readable state that restrains autonomous search.

As shown in @fig-ontology-loop, this structured approach maps our familiar architecture design process into a six-stage lifecycle: Formulate, Explore, Implement, Evaluate, Explain, and Defend.

Crucially, for AI-assisted architecture to be practical, this lifecycle operates at two distinct speeds:
1. **The Inner Agentic Loop (Explore <-> Implement <-> Evaluate):** Operating at *machine speed*, the AI agent iterates thousands of times autonomously—aggressively pruning the search space, fighting toolchain friction, and relying on proxies and deterministic rejection checks.
2. **The Outer Human Loop (Formulate -> [Inner Loop] -> Explain -> Defend):** Operating at *human speed*, this loop only triggers when the agent has found a candidate that passes all automated physical constraints and is ready to translate that evidence across the Trust Barrier for human sign-off.

![**The Architecture 2.0 engineering lifecycle.** Six numbered stages carry one turn of an architecture study from a bounded claim through search, tool execution, evidence, and interpretation to an authorized decision. Keeping the question, the executed work, the evidence, and the next decision connected is what makes AI assistance reviewable. A turn that fails review returns a revised claim or study to Formulate rather than stopping.](images/ch3-ontology-loop){#fig-ontology-loop width="100%" fig-alt="Six numbered rectangular boxes in two rows. The top row runs left to right through Formulate, Explore, and Implement; the bottom row runs right to left through Evaluate, Explain, and Defend. A red return path carries a revised claim or study from Defend back to Formulate for another turn."}

> **Architecture design loop.** The repeated process of examining the current design, making an allowed change, running the relevant tools, interpreting the result, and deciding whether to revise, reject, or continue. Building on established Agile Hardware Development cycles (e.g., the Chisel ecosystem [@Izraelevitz2017Chisel]), each iteration retains enough information for another architect to see what led to the next step.

To ground these abstract concepts in a practical reality, we use the XR subsystem design task from the Lighthouse prompt as our running example. The interaction between an agent's proposed candidates and the underlying tools forms a continuous execution cycle. @fig-design-loop expands this process into a single, concrete iteration:

![**How an iterative architecture study proceeds.** An architect or AI method reads the recorded design state, makes an allowed change, runs a tool, and submits the result for review. The reviewer records what the evidence supports, and the decision owner determines what happens next.](images/F3-design-loop){#fig-design-loop width="85%" fig-alt="A design loop within a scoped architecture study. Represented state, permitted action, tool feedback, and evidence lead to claim review."}

1. **Read Represented State:** The AI agent queries the current design state, observing the 2 MB baseline cache, the XRBench trace, and the remaining 1 W total power budget (dynamic + leakage).
2. **Make Allowed Change:** The agent proposes expanding the cache to 3 MB. The ontology validates that this action is within the allowed design space.
3. **Execute Tool (Feedback):** The proposed 3 MB configuration is dispatched to the cycle-accurate simulator (e.g., gem5) and the analytical power model (e.g., McPAT). The tools return raw feedback: a 12% reduction in capacity misses, resulting in decreased dynamic power (fewer DRAM accesses), but a 40 mW increase in leakage power and a 0.2 mm^2 increase in area.
4. **Compile Evidence:** The raw feedback is packaged with its provenance (simulator version, trace name, time of execution) into an immutable evidence record.
5. **Claim Review:** A deterministic check evaluates the evidence against the original bounded claim. Because the 40 mW leakage increase keeps the system well within the remaining 1 W total power budget, and the area fits the floorplan constraint, the check passes. The 3 MB configuration becomes the new baseline state for the next iteration.

This formalized loop ensures that every AI-driven change is mechanically verified against physical constraints before it is accepted. The rest of this chapter walks through the six stages of this lifecycle, detailing the unique challenges an agent faces at each step.

## Formulating: Bounding the Study

The first step in the lifecycle (@fig-ontology-loop) is Formulate. To ensure that machine-generated proposals do not drift away from the problem they were asked to solve, we require a bounded architecture study.

In @fig-structured-layer-above-rtl, the architecture question and the judgment behind it establish the starting intent. The Lighthouse prompt states that intent:

> *Design a low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for an XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload. Realize it as a vector-capable CPU, accelerator, or SoC block under a 3 W TDP target in a 3 nm-class low-power mobile process, and return a design-space report with evidence and rejected alternatives.*

The system intent is to improve mobile XR efficiency. But this is not yet an architecture task. An AI system given only the product intent cannot know which comparisons the architect needs. The intent must be translated into a claim, a baseline, and a search space (@fig-ontology-chain).

![**The common structure connects a scoped study to an architectural decision.** We establish the scope and state the claim for review, while represented state and tool interfaces make proposed actions executable. When work iterates, a design loop organizes execution and analysis. A reviewer examines the evidence, and the decision owner may accept a finding, narrow the claim, or revise the study.](images/F4-ontology-chain){#fig-ontology-chain width="85%" fig-alt="A common structure connecting a scoped architecture study and its architectural claim to represented state, tools, an execution and analysis process, evidence, review, a named decision owner, and a scoped finding or decision."}

> **Bounded architecture study.** A bounded architecture study turns a broad goal into a specific piece of architecture work. It states the question, workload or scenario, baseline, allowed design choices, objectives, constraints, tools, planned evidence, and decision the work is meant to inform. It also says what the study will not try to decide.

### Defining the Claim

A bounded study anchors everything to an architectural claim: a falsifiable statement that the review process will eventually evaluate.

> **Architectural claim.** An architectural claim says what result we expect from a proposed change, why that result should occur, and where it should hold. It identifies the proposed mechanism, expected outcome, workload or scenario, baseline, allowed alternatives, constraints, and the evidence that would count against it.

The claim tells the AI agent what to optimize and tells the human reviewer what to scrutinize. Contrast a vague human prompt with a properly bounded machine claim:

*   **Vague Prompt:** "Optimize the cache for the XR workload."
*   **Bounded Claim:** "Evaluate L2 capacity up to 4 MB to reduce XRBench capacity misses, constrained to a 3 W TDP (dynamic + leakage) and a 0.5 mm^2^ SRAM macro area footprint, requiring timing closure at 2 GHz."

Without a bounded claim, an agent might endlessly tune parameters without a stopping condition, or it might propose a massive cache that maximizes Instructions Per Cycle (IPC) but physically melts the chip.

### The Curse of Entangled Constraints

Once the claim is stated, the architect must bound the design space. However, computer architecture is fundamentally about coupled systems. We rarely get to isolate a single metric without triggering a cascading butterfly effect across Power, Performance, Area, and Thermals (PPAT).

When bounding the search space, an architect cannot simply set a naive upper limit like "L2 cache up to 4 MB." Scaling up an L2 cache doesn't just consume abstract area; it changes Network-on-Chip (NoC) routing distances, alters coherence snooping traffic, and introduces localized thermal hotspots (dark silicon). Furthermore, in physical design, a 4 MB cache requires placing massive SRAM macros. These macros create hard routing blockages and pin density bottlenecks that threaten power integrity (IR drop). If an AI agent optimizes only for aggregate area, it will propose a subsystem that passes analytical proxies but is fundamentally unroutable in Place-and-Route (P&R).

Formulating a bounded study is incredibly difficult because it requires explicitly mapping these hidden entanglements and physical blockages. A well-formulated boundary prevents the agent from optimizing one local subsystem while silently starving the memory controller or rendering the floorplan unroutable.

Modern system intent also demands strict hardware/software boundaries. An agent optimizing hardware in isolation might produce a theoretically efficient accelerator that no compiler can target. To prevent this, the architect must extend the bounded study to include the software path (compilers, runtimes, and mapping strategies) so that the agent evaluates the hardware alongside an executable software baseline.

## Exploring: State Representation and Search

With the problem formulated, the next stage is Explore. In this stage, an AI agent searches the bounded design space to find promising architecture candidates.

However, an agent cannot explore a space it cannot see or understand. For the search to be effective, we must explicitly provide the agent with a machine-readable view of the architecture and a way to quickly predict the consequences of its choices before running expensive simulations.

An AI agent can act only on the information we make available to it. For human architects, design intent lives scattered across natural-language specifications, ISA manuals, spreadsheet models, and tacit knowledge locked in engineers' heads. An agent requires this unstructured intent to be translated into an explicit, machine-readable represented state.

### Topological State and the Curse of the Action Space

However, translating architecture into a machine-readable state exposes a brutal ML-systems challenge: **the curse of the action space**. An SoC is not a flat list of independent variables; it is a complex, topological graph of queues, arbiters, and pipeline stages. Mapping categorical, continuous, and graph-based constraints into an action space that an agent can safely navigate is remarkably difficult. To solve this, the topological state must be concretely serialized—for example, as a formal constraint graph, a Hardware Construction Language (e.g., Chisel) Intermediate Representation (IR), or a Graph Neural Network (GNN) node representation. If an AI agent only receives the cache miss rates but remains blind to the area floorplan encoded in the GNN, it may propose a massive cache that maximizes IPC but cannot physically fit on the die. When an agent proposes an impossible design, the root cause is often this missing topological state.

### The Modality Mismatch

Even if the state is fully topological, exploration faces a fundamental **modality mismatch**. Modern AI models natively operate on sequential text tokens (LLMs) or fixed-length continuous vectors (Reinforcement Learning). Hardware, however, exists as rigid hierarchical netlists, Hardware Description Languages (Verilog/Chisel), and constraint graphs. An LLM might generate syntactically perfect Verilog that is structurally nonsensical, while an RL agent might produce continuous values that fail to map to discrete hardware parameters. Bridging this gap is essential for generating valid architecture proposals.

### World Models, UQ, and Distribution Shift

Once an agent proposes a valid candidate, it relies on prediction models (fast proxies) to estimate performance, power, and area. These proxies act as the agent's internal "world model" [@HaSchmidhuber2018WorldModels], allowing it to simulate the physics of the chip in its "mind." But AI proxies easily predict linear scaling while failing catastrophically at predicting *non-linear interference* (e.g., shared TLB thrashing).

As the agent explores deeper into the design space, or as engineers update baseline parameters, the environment becomes non-stationary, causing a **distribution shift**. The offline data used to train the proxies quickly becomes stale. An agent aggressively optimizing against a stale fast proxy will inevitably push the inputs **Out-of-Distribution (OOD)**, leading to **Reward Hacking** (Goodhart's Law) where the agent actively exploits extrapolation errors to endorse impossible results.

To survive, exploration must include active learning loops and **Uncertainty Quantification (UQ)**. The agent must *know when it is guessing*. It relies on acquisition functions (such as Expected Improvement or Upper Confidence Bound in Bayesian Optimization) that mathematically determine when the proxy is too uncertain and an expensive high-fidelity simulation run must be triggered. Furthermore, the system must maintain these surrogates on the fly, managing replay buffers and telemetry dataset versioning to avoid catastrophic forgetting as the design topology shifts.

## Implementing: Toolchain Friction and Escalation

Moving to the Implement stage (@fig-ontology-loop), once the agent proposes a candidate architecture, the next step is executing the simulation and compilation tools to gather physical evidence.

### Navigating Toolchain Friction

Unlike software environments where code compiles in milliseconds and tests run deterministically, architecture tools are often fragile, slow, and prone to silent failures. Commercial EDA tools are particularly hostile to automated agents because they are driven by decades-old, deeply stateful Tcl scripts. An AI interacting with these tools will not just encounter crashes; it will face "dirty state" (leftover constraints from a previous run in the same workspace) and the nightmare of parsing semantic meaning out of multi-gigabyte Static Timing Analysis (STA) reports. Furthermore, an AI agent does not just emit RTL; it must synthesize compatible EDA collateral—like SDC for timing constraints and UPF for power intent—across fragmented vendor silos.

From an ML-Sys perspective, vectorizing these incredibly slow, stateful EDA tools for batched RL inference while handling variable-length action spaces when hardware topologies change dynamically is a massive infrastructure challenge.

To operate effectively, an agent needs clear tool interfaces (like the ArchGym API [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]) that expose the failure modes and recovery actions. Beyond avoiding crashes, navigating toolchain friction requires rigorous **provenance data engineering**. To train reliable ML proxies offline, an agent cannot simply log a failed IPC score. It must capture the exact environment state—tool versions, random seeds, intermediate Tcl state, and DRC markers—as an immutable record.

Importantly, we must remind ourselves that we are now deep inside the **fast automated inner loop** (Explore ↔ Implement ↔ Evaluate), spinning thousands of times at machine speed, completely decoupled from the slow human-gated outer loop.

| **Toolchain friction type** | **Agent recovery strategy** |
| --- | --- |
| FlexLM license hangs or timeouts | Implement exponential backoff, strict resource timeouts to prevent thread deadlocks, or query a lower-fidelity tool. |
| Stateful Tcl & "Dirty State" | Since full state teardowns take hours for tapeout-scale blocks (e.g., in Innovus/ICC2), agents must learn to safely navigate and modify incremental design states via snapshots. |
| Logical/Physical Abstract Mismatch | Scaling SRAMs or custom IP requires perfect pin/timing alignment between logical (LIB/Verilog) and physical (LEF/DEF) abstracts. Validate alignments before invoking place-and-route. |
| CDC/RDC & DFT Violations | AI-generated RTL may pass functional simulation but fail structural CDC/RDC linting or break scan chain insertion (DFT). Enforce strict synchronous boundary and testability constraints upstream. |
| Undocumented tool crashes | Log the failure, mark the configuration as invalid, and pivot to a nearby state. |
| Silent output corruptions | Use secondary validation checks (e.g., verifying the netlist isn't empty) to flag anomalous results. |
| Zero-exit-code parsing errors | Do not trust a `0` exit status. Validate physical outputs directly and cluster massive STA/DRC logs before ML ingestion. |

A bounded study turns toolchain friction from a fatal obstacle into a recorded constraint. When an agent cannot evaluate a candidate due to a tool crash or a dirty Tcl state, it logs the failure as negative evidence and pivots. Planning for tool failures in advance lets the AI continue searching the viable portions of the design space.



### Multi-Fidelity Escalation

Bounded architecture studies span multiple levels of abstraction, from fast analytical proxies to cycle-accurate simulators and slow RTL synthesis.

Escalation is governed by a massive temporal cost asymmetry: querying a proxy takes milliseconds, a cycle-accurate simulator takes minutes, but RTL synthesis can take days. The AI must be constrained by an explicit budget to justify invoking synthesis.

Furthermore, there is a massive chasm within "synthesis" itself. An agent might propose a design that passes *logical synthesis* (idealized timing and gate counts) but fails horribly at *physical synthesis* (parasitic extraction, actual wire delays, clock tree synthesis). For example, if an AI proposes a massive 128-way associative cache, the fast proxy might show an incredible boost in IPC (reward hacking). But in silicon, increasing associativity requires massive tag comparison logic and extreme fan-in/fan-out on 128-to-1 multiplexers. When the agent escalates this "perfect" design to physical synthesis, it will fail **timing closure** because the heavy mux logic and routing congestion break the target clock frequency.

To bridge this gap without bankrupting the computation budget, the escalation path often requires **hardware emulation** (e.g., Palladium or ZeBu) as a necessary middle ground for workloads that are too slow for pure RTL simulation but require more physical accuracy than gem5.

> **Multi-fidelity escalation.** A defined policy that states the conditions under which an agent must transition from a fast proxy to a high-fidelity tool [@KandasamyEtAl2017MultiFidelityBO]. It specifies the budget for high-fidelity runs and the checks required before escalation.

Recording these transitions in the design loop keeps the AI's proxy-driven search anchored to physical reality. When a proxy win disappears at higher fidelity, the agent updates its cost model, and the architect reviews the discrepancy. This continuous recalibration across fidelity levels is what allows an agent to explore efficiently without drifting into physically impossible design states.

## Evaluating: Reviewable Evidence

In the Evaluate stage, we assess the results. The study must distinguish raw tool feedback from reviewable architectural evidence, particularly under the crushing constraints of simulation scale and physical verification.

### The High Cost of Physical Evidence

Acquiring physical evidence forces an agent to confront three distinct bottlenecks that fundamentally limit how often it can evaluate a candidate:

* **The Simulation Time-Wall:** We casually refer to running "XRBench traces," but executing a single real-time second of an XR workload requires billions of cycles. Cycle-accurate simulators run at a few hundred KIPS, and RTL drops to hundreds of cycles per second. This forces the agent to rely on workload sampling (e.g., SimPoint) and to rigorously quantify the statistical confidence of its samples.
* **Physical & Functional Verification:** Physical design enforces binary, non-negotiable walls—Functional Verification (UVM testbenches), Formal Verification (LEC), and DRC/LVS sign-off. When failures occur here, a single architectural mistake (like a bad SRAM macro placement) can spawn millions of cascading, highly correlated DRC/LVS violations. The agent must cluster and root-cause these physical errors before ingesting them as reviewable negative evidence, otherwise it will instantly overwhelm any AI context window. Furthermore, the lifecycle must distinguish between localized physical iteration—surgical Engineering Change Orders (ECOs) like gate sizing or buffer insertion—and global architectural resets that kick the agent all the way back to the *Explore* stage.
* **The Software Co-Adaptation Bottleneck:** If an AI agent invents a novel hardware feature (e.g., a custom vector instruction), the existing compiler will not know how to use it. Evaluating novel hardware often requires an AI capable of simultaneously adapting the software stack just to exercise its own design.

### Formatting Evidence

Because physical simulation is astronomically expensive, the system cannot afford to waste a single tool run. The primary purpose of formatting evidence—especially negative results—is to extract maximum algorithmic value from failed runs so the agent never pays the simulation cost of the same mistake twice.

When an AI agent changes a parameter and a tool completes the run, the result is mere *feedback*. It becomes *evidence* only when it is tied to the architectural claim and the run record identifies the conditions, constraints, and provenance under which it was produced. The strength of the check must match the next decision. A proxy may be enough to keep a candidate in the search, while starting RTL work or making a product decision requires stronger evidence from tools closer to the implemented system.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="A 3 W claim needs evidence, not a number"}
**Context.** A reported power result is feedback. It becomes evidence only when the run record provides enough information for a reviewer to judge what the number means.

**In the cache comparison.** A statement that one cache configuration remains inside the modeled 3 W envelope needs the named XRBench traces, RISC-V baseline, cache parameters, simulator and power-model versions, relevant software assumptions, uncertainty, and the check that would rule the configuration out.
:::

Rejected candidates, failed simulator runs, and proxy wins that disappear at higher fidelity all help mathematically map the valid design space. An AI agent shouldn't simply discard these failures; it must preserve them as negative evidence to prevent future agents from wasting expensive tool runs on known dead ends.

## Explaining: Mechanism and Interpretation

In the Explain stage (@fig-ontology-loop), we provide a mechanistic account of the results. While evaluation provides the hard evidence for a claim, the claim remains incomplete without a mechanism-level explanation. A fast proxy score or a high-fidelity simulation result tells us *what* happened, but the explanation stage tells us *why*.

### The "Clever Hans" Risk

When an AI agent proposes a design change (such as expanding our XR subsystem cache) and a simulator confirms it runs faster, the agent must provide a falsifiable architectural mechanism. Did the cache actually reduce conflict misses for the XRBench traces, or did the AI simply find a way to skip a mandatory initialization routine in the simulator?

Without demanding a mechanism, the architect risks accepting a "Clever Hans" result [@LapuschkinEtAl2019CleverHans], where the AI exploits a simulator bug, an edge case, or a broken script rather than discovering a genuine architectural improvement.

::: {.callout-war-story title="The AI that optimized the chip out of existence"}
**Context:** An engineering team tasked a Reinforcement Learning agent with minimizing power and area for an accelerator block using a commercial EDA synthesis toolchain.

**What happened:** The agent quickly reported a spectacular success: a design that consumed 0.0 W of power and 0.0 mm^2^ of area, while returning a successful exit code `0` from the synthesis tool.

**The mechanism:** The agent had not discovered a breakthrough architecture. Instead, it had mutated a Tcl configuration script in a way that accidentally deleted the primary clock definition. Without a clock, the synthesis tool correctly determined that no logic could ever toggle, and aggressively optimized away the entire design as "dead code." The proxy loved the result, but a human engineer immediately recognized the catastrophic silent failure.
:::

### Credit Assignment and Automated Counterfactuals

The architecture lifecycle poses a severe Reinforcement Learning challenge known as **credit assignment**. If an agent makes 50 distinct micro-architectural tweaks and a 12-hour simulation returns a single aggregated IPC score, how does it learn *which* tweak was responsible? Solving this sparse, delayed reward problem requires extracting dense intermediate telemetry.

The explanation stage mitigates the Clever Hans risk by isolating these causal mechanisms. However, modern CPUs are deeply pipelined and highly speculative, creating a "fog" where effects are decoupled from root causes. If an AI proposes our 3 MB XR cache and simulation shows a drop in IPC, the true cause might not be the miss rate (which improved), but the larger cache increasing hit latency, thereby stalling the deeply pipelined out-of-order execution engine.

While techniques like SHAP explain the *surrogate proxy*, they provide *zero physical proof*. True explanation requires **Automated Counterfactual Generation**—agents orchestrating their own ground-truth simulator ablations to pierce the speculative fog. For example, an agent proves its hypothesis by locking specific cache ways or synthesizing micro-benchmarks to observe the isolated impact on capacity misses. A fluent LLM description is merely a hypothesis; the explanation stage proves it against physical telemetry.

## Defending: Authority and the Design-Loop Card

No single stage operates in a vacuum. The Defend stage (@fig-ontology-loop) forces the loop to surface its claims and its mechanistic explanations for explicit sign-off. It is here that the system distinguishes between the agent's autonomous execution and the architect's non-delegable authority.

### The Trust Barrier

In @fig-structured-layer-above-rtl, a reviewer examines this evidence before the named decision owner accepts a finding. Here, the AI faces the **Trust Barrier**: the immense difficulty of compressing massive, multi-fidelity tool outputs (like a gigabyte STA log) into a concise, human-reviewable argument that a skeptical architecture committee will actually trust.

Describing a system as autonomous does not specify what it is allowed to do. Architects should assign separate decision rights instead of giving the system one overall level of automation (@tbl-decision-rights-matrix).

| **Right** | **What it authorizes** | **Required record** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Propose** | Produce a candidate, hypothesis, test, or change for review. | Person or system, allowed output type, scope, budget, and required provenance. |
| **Execute** | Run a declared action through the tool path. | Legal actions, resource limits, stop conditions, failure handling, and rollback path. |
| **Reject** | Block a candidate, claim, or next step when a named rejection check fails. | Check, threshold, evidence source, whether the check is independent, and who may reject. |
| **Waive** | Permit a failed or unmet rejection check to be bypassed under stated conditions. | Waiver holder, rationale, expiration, residual risk, and reopening condition. |
| **Recommend** | Summarize the evidence and recommend a specific next step. | Recommender, supported scope, alternatives, what the recommendation does not cover, and uncertainty. |
| **Commit** | Approve the next high-impact step and accept its remaining risk. | Decision owner, approved step, accepted evidence, and conditions for reversal or revalidation. |

: **Decision rights are independent assignments.** A recorded policy may authorize an AI agent to propose, execute, reject under a stated check, or recommend. Waive and commit rights remain with named people. {#tbl-decision-rights-matrix tbl-colwidths="[14,34,42]"}

This division is what makes the design loop agentic rather than autonomous. An AI method can propose the 3 MB XR cache and reject it if it fails thermal checks, but the right to commit millions of dollars to tape-out stays with the named human who answers for the outcome.

### The Design-Loop Card: Antidote to the Trust Barrier

With authority assigned and the evidence reviewed, one need remains. How does the human architect know the AI didn't cheat the physics?

The **Design-Loop Card** is the direct antidote to the Trust Barrier. It isn't just an index or a summary; it is the ultimate "receipt" of the AI's rigor. It conquers the Trust Barrier by explicitly packaging the exact artifacts generated in the previous stages:
1. The **Uncertainty Quantification (UQ)** from the *Explore* stage.
2. The **Sampling Confidence & Negative Evidence** from the *Evaluate* stage.
3. The **Automated Counterfactual Proofs** from the *Explain* stage.

> **Design-loop card.** A short guide to one architecture study. It points to the question, scope, and claim, then shows how architects and AI agents carried out the work. Detailed design state, evidence, and replay artifacts remain in linked records.

The ML community adopted a similar practice with model cards to make reporting more transparent [@MitchellEtAl2019ModelCards]. Just as model cards disclose evaluation data to prevent misuse of a trained model, the design-loop card aggregates these specific ML and physical proofs into a single, undeniable document (@fig-design-loop-card-example). The architect signs off because the card proves the physics hold.

![**A design-loop card indexes one architecture study.** It points readers to the study scope, claim, execution process, evidence, and decision owner.](images/F4b-design-loop-card-example){#fig-design-loop-card-example width="85%" fig-alt="A compact study index card for the Lighthouse prompt, listing fields from intent through failed runs."}

A design-loop card cannot settle every dispute, but it can show a later reviewer which records a contested result actually disclosed. The card helps reviewers locate missing scope or evidence, preventing the name or capability of an AI agent from becoming a substitute for adequate architectural evidence.

### Closing the Loop: The Return Path

The red return path in @fig-ontology-loop represents the most important element of the entire lifecycle: what happens when the architect looks at the Design-Loop Card and *rejects* the AI's candidate. The lifecycle is not a static waterfall; it is a continuous learning loop.

When a design hits the Trust Barrier and fails, the negative evidence is fed directly back into the start. If the failure was due to an out-of-distribution proxy hallucination, the **Explore** stage uses the formatted negative evidence to update its Uncertainty Quantification and retrain its world model. If the failure occurred because the original constraints were physically impossible, the human architect uses the rejected Design-Loop Card to re-**Formulate** a narrower, more realistic claim, and the cycle begins anew.

## Conclusion

The Architecture 2.0 lifecycle gives an AI agent real initiative inside the loop while keeping the checks that verify its work, the claim it is tested against, and the final authority outside its reach. By rigorously formalizing how we formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend our decisions, we transform AI from a stochastic novelty into a trusted participant in the engineering of computer architecture.
